title: Black Coffee on Cotton
stainChemistry: Black coffee is primarily tannins (no cream protein). Tannins bond to cotton's cellulose through hydrogen attraction. White cotton's chemical tolerance allows aggressive acid and oxidizing treatment. Fresh coffee is easier to remove than brown/aged (oxidized) coffee. Acidic chemistry breaks tannin-cellulose bonds directly—this is the key advantage vs protein stains.
whyThisWorks: Acetic acid breaks tannin hydrogen bonds to cotton. H₂O₂ or bleach oxidizes tannin pigment. Sequential chemistry (acid then oxidizer) removes both the stain and its color efficiently.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Cold water from back, blot hard. This removes excess coffee and mobilizes tannins before they oxidize deeper. Most fresh stain will flush out with immediate cold water blotting. Flip to front and continue. Water should turn brown/tan.

Apply 28% Acetic Acid (diluted) directly to remaining brown stain. Full strength is safe on white cotton. Acidity breaks tannin-cellulose bonds. Watch the stain lighten. After 2-3 minutes, blot thoroughly. The acidic chemistry is what makes coffee tannins soluble.

If brown color remains, apply H₂O₂. This oxidizes tannin pigment to colorless products. Full strength safe on white cotton. Chemical smell sharp—oxidation happening. After 3-5 minutes, blot thoroughly.

Flush thoroughly with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) to remove all chemistry residue. Work from back of fabric. Blot — do not rub. All oxidizer and enzyme residue must be fully removed before drying.
Rinse thoroughly to remove all acid, peroxide, and H₂O₂ for white/light fabrics only. Then wash in warm or hot water with regular detergent. All chemistry must be rinsed away.

homeSolutions: FRESH: Cold water flush from back. Blot hard. Most comes out immediately.
REMAINING: white vinegar, 2-3 minutes, blot. Then H₂O₂, 3-5 minutes, blot.
BROWN: OxiClean soak (30-60 min) or dilute bleach (1:10, white only, 5-10 min).
materialWarnings: DO NOT use bleach on colored cotton
escalation: Black coffee on white cotton rarely needs professional care. Home treatment is fast and effective.

household.name: White Vinegar
household.note: Acidic spotter for tannin. Full strength safe on white cotton. DO NOT substitute industrial-strength (28%) acetic acid — it causes chemical burns.

household.name: H₂O₂ (3-6%)
household.note: Oxidizes tannin color. Full strength safe on white cotton.

household.name: OxiClean or Clorox Bleach
household.note: OxiClean safe on colored trim. Bleach (1:10 diluted) fast on white only.

professional.name: StreeTAN
professional.note: Professional acetic acid spotter. Rarely needed—home vinegar works well.

customerExplanation: Black coffee contains tannins that bond to cotton. Acid (vinegar) breaks the bonds. H₂O₂ oxidizes the brown color to colorless. White cotton's chemical robustness means we can use strong chemistry and heat safely.
commonMistakes: Not using cold water first—initial flush removes most of the stain
Skipping acid—vinegar is essential for breaking tannin bonds
Not using peroxide—it's the final step that removes brown color completely
scienceNote: Black coffee tannins are complex polyphenolic compounds. Fresh tannins are more soluble and lighter in color (tan/medium brown). As they oxidize (gain oxygen bridges), they darken (dark brown) and become less soluble. This is why fresh stains respond to acid alone, but aged stains need oxidizing chemistry. Bleach actually accelerates tannin oxidation to colorless products—this is why bleach works so well on aged coffee on white cotton.
callPro: Black coffee on white cotton rarely needs professional care.
solventNote: Professional: Cold water flush, then acetic acid spotter for fresh tannins. For aged brown stains, H₂O₂ (3-6%) or sodium percarbonate. For very old stains, chlorine bleach (0.5-1% on white, dilute in water) or reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite 0.5%, warm, 10-15 min) to reverse oxidation, then oxidize to colorless. Standard approach: acid → oxidize → oxidize again.
aftercare: White cotton tolerates heat. Machine dryer or line dry—both fine. Heat doesn't re-set tannin stains on cotton.
